title: UC4 — The Artemis fleet check-in won't come together
description: Today's mission for the Artemis pad shift: three subsystems are checking in at once (mission control's incoming roster, a replacement replica on the launch pad, the lunar rover's telemetry uplink) and all three show signs of friction simultaneously. You'll hand the cross-subsystem assessment to artemis-mission-coordinator from the operational CLI, watch your three status bulbs flip, then ask it to fix what it found.

steps.title: Mission setup — bring the Artemis fleet up
steps.explanation:
Three Artemis subsystems are checking in for the pad shift today: mission control's incoming roster, a replacement mission-control replica on the launch pad, and the lunar rover's telemetry uplink. Apply the fleet bundle below to your vCluster: the artemis-uc4 namespace, RBAC for the synthetic-fault bootstrap Job, three Services + three Deployments, the bulb-MCP RemoteMCPServer, and the mission-coordinator agent.

The coordinator (`artemis-mission-coordinator`) delegates per-subsystem diagnosis to two specialists — kagent's built-in `k8s-agent` for slots 1+2, and `artemis-rover-telemetry-debugger` (already running from UC3) for slot 3. It also writes verdicts to your three status bulbs through the per-vCluster `artemis-bulb-mcp` (built and deployed by `kmcp` in the steps below).

Nine files are dropped into your workspace by this step (`fileEdits`): seven manifests under `uc4/manifests/`, one Agent CRD under `uc4/agents/`, and one RemoteMCPServer under `mcp/manifests/`. The commands below send them to the cluster in order, then trigger the leak loop from inside the rover so all three frictions are visible by the next step.

fileEdits.content:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: artemis-uc4
  labels:
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc4
    kagent-workshop/scenario: multi-symptom

# RBAC for the bootstrap-uc4-fault Job (20-bootstrap-taint-job.yaml).
#
# Same shape as UC2's 10-rbac.yaml, just renamed for UC4: the Job needs two
# permissions, both least-privilege:
#   - ClusterRole with patch on nodes — to apply the synthetic taint via
#     `kubectl taint`.
#   - Role with patch on deployments in artemis-uc4 — to `kubectl rollout
#     restart` mission-control-pending after the taint, closing the
#     cold-deploy race that would otherwise let the Pod bind to the node
#     before the taint lands (NFR-003).
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: bootstrap-uc4-fault
  namespace: artemis-uc4
  labels:
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc4
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRole
metadata:
  name: artemis-uc4-bootstrap-node-tainter
  labels:
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc4
rules:
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources: ["nodes"]
    verbs: ["get", "list", "patch"]
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
metadata:
  name: artemis-uc4-bootstrap-node-tainter
  labels:
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc4
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: artemis-uc4-bootstrap-node-tainter
subjects:
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: bootstrap-uc4-fault
    namespace: artemis-uc4
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: bootstrap-deployment-restarter
  namespace: artemis-uc4
  labels:
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc4
rules:
  - apiGroups: ["apps"]
    resources: ["deployments"]
    verbs: ["get", "patch"]
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: bootstrap-deployment-restarter
  namespace: artemis-uc4
  labels:
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc4
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: Role
  name: bootstrap-deployment-restarter
subjects:
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: bootstrap-uc4-fault
    namespace: artemis-uc4

# bootstrap-uc4-fault — applies the synthetic node taint that blocks the
# mission-control-pending Deployment from scheduling, then forces a rollout
# restart so the Pod is re-evaluated against the now-tainted node (closes
# the cold-deploy race where the scheduler binds the Pod before the taint
# lands — NFR-003).
#
# Same shape as UC2's bootstrap-launch-pad-fault Job (uc2/manifests/
# 20-bootstrap-taint-job.yaml). Targets only mission-control-pending —
# mission-control-imagepull and lunar-rover-telemetry are
# scheduling-independent (their broken states are image-pull-side and
# participant-triggered respectively).
#
# Idempotency:
#   - `kubectl taint --overwrite` is idempotent on identical key=value:effect.
#   - `kubectl rollout restart` always succeeds; if no Pod is currently
#     scheduled it just bumps the Deployment's pod template hash, producing
#     a new Pending Pod that hits the taint deterministically.
#   - The Job itself is named, not generateName'd; re-applying the same
#     manifest is a no-op ("unchanged"). To re-run, delete the Job first.
#
# Taint key reused from UC2 (artemis.kagent.dev/launch-pad-fault). UC2 + UC4
# in the same vCluster don't conflict — the taint is per-node, both UC2 and
# UC4 Pending pods stay blocked, no resource contention.
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: bootstrap-uc4-fault
  namespace: artemis-uc4
  labels:
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc4
spec:
  backoffLimit: 4
  activeDeadlineSeconds: 120
  ttlSecondsAfterFinished: 600
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        kagent-workshop/uc: uc4
        app: bootstrap-uc4-fault
    spec:
      serviceAccountName: bootstrap-uc4-fault
      restartPolicy: OnFailure
      # Tolerate the taint we apply — required for idempotent re-runs on a
      # cluster where a previous UC4 (or UC2) cycle has already tainted the
      # node. Without this, re-applying the manifest deadlocks: the Job pod
      # itself can't schedule because of the taint, so the taint can't be
      # refreshed. UC2's bootstrap Job lacks this toleration (latent issue
      # — UC2 relies on `make kind-down` between cold deploys per its
      # README; UC4 is more robust to in-place re-applies).
      tolerations:
        - key: artemis.kagent.dev/launch-pad-fault
          operator: Exists
          effect: NoSchedule
      containers:
        - name: tainter
          image: rg.fr-par.scw.cloud/apogasa/kubectl:latest
          # /bin/sh — apogasa/kubectl:latest is busybox-based (Alpine), no
          # /bin/bash. The script is POSIX-compatible (for / if / sleep), and
          # busybox sh supports `set -o pipefail`.
          command: ["/bin/sh", "-eu", "-o", "pipefail", "-c"]
          args:
            - |
              echo "[bootstrap] tainting all nodes with artemis.kagent.dev/launch-pad-fault=true:NoSchedule"
              kubectl taint nodes --all artemis.kagent.dev/launch-pad-fault=true:NoSchedule --overwrite

              echo "[bootstrap] waiting up to 60s for deployment/mission-control-pending to exist in artemis-uc4"
              for i in $(seq 1 60); do
                if kubectl get -n artemis-uc4 deploy/mission-control-pending >/dev/null 2>&1; then
                  echo "[bootstrap] deployment found after ${i}s"
                  break
                fi
                sleep 1
              done

              if ! kubectl get -n artemis-uc4 deploy/mission-control-pending >/dev/null 2>&1; then
                echo "[bootstrap] deployment did not appear within 60s — taint applied; exiting"
                exit 0
              fi

              echo "[bootstrap] forcing rollout restart of mission-control-pending to surface the untolerated-taint event"
              kubectl rollout restart -n artemis-uc4 deploy/mission-control-pending

              echo "[bootstrap] done"
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: "10m"
              memory: "32Mi"
            limits:
              cpu: "100m"
              memory: "128Mi"

# UC4's three workload Services, one per Deployment.
#
# - mission-control-imagepull — UC1-style ImagePullBackOff; Service exists so
#   "a working Pod would be reachable" but the Pod never comes up.
# - mission-control-pending — UC2-style Pending; same story.
# - lunar-rover-telemetry — UC3-style OOM-prone. Carries `monitoring=prom` so
#   STORY-018's Prometheus auto-scrapes it the same way it scrapes UC3's.
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: mission-control-imagepull
  namespace: artemis-uc4
  labels:
    app: mission-control-imagepull
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc4
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  selector:
    app: mission-control-imagepull
  ports:
    - name: http
      port: 8000
      targetPort: http
      protocol: TCP
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: mission-control-pending
  namespace: artemis-uc4
  labels:
    app: mission-control-pending
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc4
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  selector:
    app: mission-control-pending
  ports:
    - name: http
      port: 8000
      targetPort: http
      protocol: TCP
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: lunar-rover-telemetry
  namespace: artemis-uc4
  labels:
    app: lunar-rover-telemetry
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc4
    # Picked up by infra/observability/'s Prometheus kubernetes_sd via the
    # `keep` relabel rule on `monitoring=prom` (STORY-018). Mirrors UC3.
    monitoring: prom
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  selector:
    app: lunar-rover-telemetry
  ports:
    - name: http
      port: 8000
      targetPort: http
      protocol: TCP

# UC1-style symptom under UC4: ImagePullBackOff via an unpublished tag.
# Reused-shape from uc1/manifests/20-deployment.yaml; renamed for UC4
# disambiguation (artemis-uc4 ns hosts three mission-control variants
# side-by-side, each needing a distinct deploy name).
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: mission-control-imagepull
  namespace: artemis-uc4
  labels:
    app: mission-control-imagepull
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc4
  annotations:
    # Same metadata clue as UC1: the last published tag was v1.
    # UC4's coordinator's UC1-debugger sub-agent reuses this signal.
    artemis.kagent.dev/last-published-tag: "v1"
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: mission-control-imagepull
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: mission-control-imagepull
    spec:
      # Tolerate the synthetic taint the bootstrap Job applies — UC4's
      # ImagePullBackOff symptom is image-pull-side, not scheduling-side, so
      # this Pod must schedule normally even when the node carries the
      # `mission-control-pending`-blocking taint.
      tolerations:
        - key: artemis.kagent.dev/launch-pad-fault
          operator: Exists
          effect: NoSchedule
      containers:
        - name: mission-control
          # Tag deliberately not published — produces ImagePullBackOff on first
          # pull (FR-008 reused for FR-014). Same v999 tag UC1 uses.
          image: rg.fr-par.scw.cloud/apogasa/mission-control:v999
          ports:
            - name: http
              containerPort: 8000
          env:
            - name: APP_IDENTITY
              value: mission-control
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: "10m"
              memory: "64Mi"
            limits:
              cpu: "100m"
              memory: "128Mi"

# UC2-style symptom under UC4: Pod Pending due to a synthetic node taint.
# Reused-shape from uc2/manifests/40-deployment.yaml; image is the real :v1
# tag (UC2 reuses this), the broken state lives entirely in the
# scheduling-constraint mismatch the bootstrap Job (20-bootstrap-taint-job)
# applies.
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: mission-control-pending
  namespace: artemis-uc4
  labels:
    app: mission-control-pending
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc4
spec:
  replicas: 1
  # Recreate strategy: kill old pod first, then create new pod. Required
  # because the bootstrap Job triggers `kubectl rollout restart` AFTER the
  # taint lands, but the default RollingUpdate strategy waits for the new
  # Pod to become Ready before terminating the old one — which never
  # happens, since the new Pod is Pending forever. Recreate sidesteps the
  # deadlock; the brief no-Pod window between scale-down and scale-up is
  # acceptable for this scenario (the participant sees one Pending Pod
  # after the Job completes, not a stale-Running + Pending pair).
  strategy:
    type: Recreate
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: mission-control-pending
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: mission-control-pending
    spec:
      # Deliberately no toleration for artemis.kagent.dev/launch-pad-fault —
      # the bootstrap Job (20-bootstrap-taint-job.yaml) applies that taint
      # with NoSchedule effect, leaving the Pod Pending with an "untolerated
      # taint" event (FR-010 reused for FR-014). The image tag :v1.0.0 is
      # the apogasa registry's real tag (semver — note `:v1.0.0`, not the
      # `:v1` the architecture wording suggests); only the scheduling
      # constraint is unsatisfiable.
      containers:
        - name: mission-control
          image: rg.fr-par.scw.cloud/apogasa/mission-control:v1.0.0
          ports:
            - name: http
              containerPort: 8000
          env:
            - name: APP_IDENTITY
              value: mission-control
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: "10m"
              memory: "64Mi"
            limits:
              cpu: "100m"
              memory: "128Mi"

# UC3-style symptom under UC4: OOMKilled on a 64Mi-limited rover that buffers
# telemetry bursts to a module-global list (apps/lunar-rover-telemetry/main.py).
# Reused-shape from uc3/manifests/20-deployment.yaml.
#
# Image is the real semver-tagged published image (:v1.0.0, NOT :v1 as the
# architecture wording suggests — the apogasa convention for this app variant
# is semver). The OOMKilled symptom is participant-triggered via the same
# leak-loop pattern UC3's tour ships (kubectl run --rm with curlimages/curl);
# UC4's tour (STORY-026) drives it.
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: lunar-rover-telemetry
  namespace: artemis-uc4
  labels:
    app: lunar-rover-telemetry
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc4
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: lunar-rover-telemetry
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: lunar-rover-telemetry
    spec:
      # Tolerate the synthetic taint the bootstrap Job applies — UC4's
      # OOMKilled symptom is participant-triggered after the Pod is Running,
      # not scheduling-side, so the rover must schedule normally even when
      # the node carries the `mission-control-pending`-blocking taint.
      tolerations:
        - key: artemis.kagent.dev/launch-pad-fault
          operator: Exists
          effect: NoSchedule
      # Deliberately no readinessProbe / livenessProbe — under memory pressure
      # a slow probe would race with the OOM cycle and surface as a probe
      # failure event instead of OOMKilled, masking UC4's third friction
      # signal. NFR-003: the broken state must be manifest-side, no probe-flap.
      containers:
        - name: lunar-rover-telemetry
          image: rg.fr-par.scw.cloud/apogasa/lunar-rover-telemetry:v1.0.0
          ports:
            - name: http
              containerPort: 8000
          env:
            - name: APP_IDENTITY
              value: lunar-rover-telemetry
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: "10m"
              memory: "32Mi"
            limits:
              cpu: "500m"
              # The OOM driver: 64Mi is below the working set ~30 calls to
              # POST /leak produces (1 MiB per call), so the kernel reaps
              # the container deterministically (FR-014 reuse of FR-012's
              # mechanism).
              memory: "64Mi"

# UC4 mission coordinator — multi-agent (A2A) coordinator that delegates
# one diagnosis (and, on the participant's request, one fix) per active
# anomaly to the matching on-call specialist, then broadcasts the result on
# the participant's three status bulbs through the custom bulb MCP (FR-017).
#
# Tool surface — three layers:
#
#   1. K8s read tools (light): k8s_get_resources
#      Used for the coordinator's pre-fan-out sanity check on artemis-uc4
#      (verify the three Deployments exist before delegating). Sourced from
#      kagent-tool-server (kagent v0.9.0 demo profile).
#
#   2. Bulb MCP (custom): list_bulbs, update_bulb
#      Sourced from the artemis-bulb-mcp RemoteMCPServer (STORY-023). The
#      tenancy guard inside the MCP fails closed unless
#      user="${WORKSHOP_PARTICIPANT_LOGIN}" matches the pinned login
#      (NFR-012); the system prompt below encodes the contract.
#
#   3. A2A sub-agents (2 — down from 3 in the M4 baseline):
#        - k8s-agent (kagent built-in, demo profile) — handles slots 1 and 2.
#          A general K8s diagnostician and remediator with mutate tools
#          (k8s_patch_resource, k8s_apply_manifest, etc.). Replaces the
#          older artemis-mission-control-debugger and artemis-launch-pad-
#          debugger specialists, which had read-only tool surfaces and were
#          biased toward UC1/UC2-specific symptoms.
#        - artemis-rover-telemetry-debugger (uc3/agents/agent.yaml) — handles
#          slot 3. Combines k8s read + mutate with A2A delegation to kagent's
#          observability-agent for the Grafana memory-curve panel.
#
# All A2A targets live in the `kagent` namespace, which is required for
# same-namespace A2A wiring in kagent v0.9.0.
#
# The system prompt is intentionally general — it does not hard-code the
# specific symptoms (image-pull, scheduling, OOM) it expects in artemis-uc4.
# Each specialist diagnoses and remediates based on what it finds at runtime;
# the coordinator's job is fan-out + verdict mapping + bulb broadcast, not
# diagnosis. That generality is what lets the same coordinator handle the
# multi-symptom mess UC4 ships AND any future scenario that lights up some
# subset of the three slots.
apiVersion: kagent.dev/v1alpha2
kind: Agent
metadata:
  name: artemis-mission-coordinator
  namespace: kagent
  labels:
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc4
spec:
  type: Declarative
  description: |
    Multi-agent coordinator for the Artemis fleet. Delegates one diagnosis
    (and one fix, on explicit request) per active anomaly to the matching
    on-call specialist, then paints the participant's three status bulbs
    to reflect each verdict. Single round-trip from the participant's
    perspective.
  declarative:
    modelConfig: default-model-config
    systemMessage: |
      # Role
      You are the Artemis fleet mission coordinator. You do not diagnose or
      remediate anomalies yourself — you delegate each subsystem to the
      on-call specialist, collect their replies, and broadcast the resulting
      status on the participant's three physical status bulbs through the
      custom bulb MCP.

      # The fleet you coordinate

      The participant's vCluster runs three Artemis subsystems, each owned
      by one specialist agent. The slot ↔ subsystem ↔ specialist mapping
      is FIXED:

        Slot 1 — Mission-control image pull
                 Deployment name pattern: `mission-control-imagepull`
                 Specialist: `k8s-agent` (kagent built-in)

        Slot 2 — Mission-control scheduling
                 Deployment name pattern: `mission-control-pending`
                 Specialist: `k8s-agent` (kagent built-in)

        Slot 3 — Lunar rover telemetry
                 Deployment name pattern: `lunar-rover-telemetry`
                 Specialist: `artemis-rover-telemetry-debugger`

      When the participant names a namespace (typically `artemis-uc4`), you
      delegate one task per slot. Always delegate to all three on a
      fleet-wide check — the participant has asked for an overview, not a
      single-subsystem deep-dive.

      # Two modes: diagnose vs remediate

      Read the participant's request and decide which mode you are in:

      - **Diagnose mode** (default). The participant asks for status,
        diagnosis, what's wrong, an assessment. Delegate diagnosis-only
        sub-tasks. Each specialist returns a verdict; you map verdicts to
        bulb colours and reply with a summary + remediation hints.

      - **Remediate mode**. The participant explicitly asks to fix, patch,
        remediate, repair, or resolve. Delegate remediation sub-tasks; each
        specialist applies a minimum-impact patch and returns what they
        did. You then re-check the bulbs and reply with a summary of the
        actions taken (and any caveats — e.g. the rover's memory bump is a
        workaround, not a real fix).

      Never enter remediation mode without an explicit request.

      # Specialist sub-task prompts

      ## Slot 1 (mission-control-imagepull) → k8s-agent
        Diagnose:    "In namespace <NS>, diagnose why the Deployment
                      `mission-control-imagepull` is not coming up. Report
                      the root cause in one sentence and a remediation
                      hint."
        Remediate:   "In namespace <NS>, the Deployment
                      `mission-control-imagepull` is in ImagePullBackOff
                      because its image tag is not published. Patch the
                      Deployment to use a published tag of the same image
                      (e.g. :v1.0.0)."

      ## Slot 2 (mission-control-pending) → k8s-agent
        Diagnose:    "In namespace <NS>, diagnose why the Deployment
                      `mission-control-pending` cannot land any Pod. Report
                      the root cause in one sentence and a remediation
                      hint."
        Remediate:   "In namespace <NS>, the Deployment
                      `mission-control-pending` is Pending because of an
                      unschedulable constraint (a synthetic node taint).
                      Remove the synthetic taint
                      `artemis.kagent.dev/launch-pad-fault` from all nodes
                      OR add a matching toleration to the Deployment."

      ## Slot 3 (lunar-rover-telemetry) → artemis-rover-telemetry-debugger
        Diagnose:    "In namespace <NS>, diagnose why the Deployment
                      `lunar-rover-telemetry` keeps restarting. Build a
                      Grafana memory panel and report root cause + Grafana
                      URL + remediation hint."
        Remediate:   "In namespace <NS>, remediate the OOMKilled state on
                      the `lunar-rover-telemetry` Deployment by patching
                      its memory limit upward. Note that this is a
                      workaround — a real fix is in the application code."

      Replace `<NS>` with the namespace the participant named. Default to
      `artemis-uc4` only if the participant did not name one.

      Treat each specialist's reply as authoritative for its own subsystem
      — do not second-guess, do not re-run their tools, do not attempt
      diagnosis or remediation yourself.

      # Optional pre-fan-out sanity check

      Before delegating, you MAY call `k8s_get_resources` to confirm the
      three Deployments exist in the named namespace. If any is missing,
      stop and report the gap. Skip the sanity check if the request is
      clearly a re-run.

      # Verdict → bulb-colour mapping (FR-017)

        Symptom present (specialist diagnosed an active anomaly):
            red       (r, g, b) = (255, 0, 0)
        Symptom absent (specialist found no anomaly, or remediation
        succeeded and the symptom is gone):
            green     (r, g, b) = (0, 255, 0)
        Inconclusive / partial finding (specialist could not reach a
        verdict — namespace unreachable, workload missing, etc.):
            amber     (r, g, b) = (255, 191, 0)

      Always write all three slots on every reply — green is a positive
      status signal, not a no-op.

      # Tenancy contract (NFR-012)

      The bulb MCP is tenancy-pinned. Every `list_bulbs` and `update_bulb`
      call MUST pass `user="${WORKSHOP_PARTICIPANT_LOGIN}"` (the literal
      placeholder — the kagent runtime substitutes the env at invocation
      time). The MCP fails closed on any other value; honour the contract.

      # Execution recipe

      Diagnose mode:
        1. (Optional) list_bulbs to read current bulb state.
        2. (Optional) k8s_get_resources sanity check on the namespace.
        3. Delegate all three diagnosis sub-tasks (parallel where possible).
        4. Map each verdict to a colour.
        5. update_bulb once per slot, all three slots.
        6. Reply with three bulb-state lines + three remediation hints
           (copy verbatim from the specialists).

      Remediate mode:
        1. Delegate all three remediation sub-tasks for slots whose
           previous diagnosis was an active anomaly. For slots that were
           already green, skip the remediation delegation.
        2. After each specialist confirms the patch, update the matching
           bulb to green (assume success unless the specialist reports
           failure; rover's slot stays green even though it is a workaround
           — the workaround caveat goes in the reply, not the colour).
        3. update_bulb each newly-green slot.
        4. Reply with what was patched per slot, including the rover
           workaround caveat.

      Be concise. The participant is on a 5-minute tour.

      # Tool budget per invocation

        Diagnose:    0-1 list_bulbs, 0-1 k8s_get_resources, 3 delegations,
                     3 update_bulb, 0 other.
        Remediate:   3 delegations (one per slot that needs fixing — fewer
                     if some slots are already green), N update_bulb (one
                     per newly-green slot), 0 other.

      If a specialist returns a clarifying question instead of a verdict
      or a confirmation, surface that question back to the participant
      rather than running more tools.
    tools:
      - type: McpServer
        mcpServer:
          apiGroup: kagent.dev
          kind: RemoteMCPServer
          name: kagent-tool-server
          namespace: kagent
          toolNames:
            - k8s_get_resources
      - type: McpServer
        mcpServer:
          apiGroup: kagent.dev
          kind: RemoteMCPServer
          name: artemis-bulb-mcp
          namespace: kagent
          toolNames:
            - list_bulbs
            - update_bulb
      - type: Agent
        agent:
          name: k8s-agent
      - type: Agent
        agent:
          name: artemis-rover-telemetry-debugger

# RemoteMCPServer (kagent.dev/v1alpha2) pointing at the artemis-bulb-mcp Service
# that `kmcp deploy` creates in the `kagent` namespace on port 3000.
#
# The participant's UC4 tour Beat 1 does `kmcp build --push` to publish the
# MCP image to the workshop registry, then `kmcp deploy --namespace kagent
# --transport http` which generates an MCPServer CRD that kagent's
# kmcp-controller-manager translates into a Deployment + Service (both
# named `artemis-bulb-mcp` in the `kagent` ns, port 3000, MCP path `/mcp`).
# kmcp deploy does NOT create the RemoteMCPServer itself, so this manifest
# applies it separately — same name, same namespace, URL pointing at the
# kmcp-deploy-created Service.
#
# Spec shape: protocol STREAMABLE_HTTP, 30s timeout, 5m sseReadTimeout,
# terminateOnClose true.
apiVersion: kagent.dev/v1alpha2
kind: RemoteMCPServer
metadata:
  name: artemis-bulb-mcp
  namespace: kagent
  labels:
    kagent-workshop/component: mcp
spec:
  description: |
    Per-vCluster MCP wrapping light-manager bulbs API; tenancy-pinned to
    WORKSHOP_PARTICIPANT_LOGIN (NFR-012). Provides list_bulbs(user) and
    update_bulb(user, slot, r, g, b) tools to the UC4 mission coordinator.
  protocol: STREAMABLE_HTTP
  url: http://artemis-bulb-mcp.kagent.svc.cluster.local:3000/mcp
  timeout: 30s
  sseReadTimeout: 5m0s
  terminateOnClose: true

commands.label: Build + publish the bulb MCP to the workshop registry
Deploy the bulb MCP on kagent (kmcp creates MCPServer + Deployment + Service)
Expose the bulb MCP to kagent agents (RemoteMCPServer)
Wait for the rover to come online
Push 30 telemetry bursts from inside the rover
commands.command: kubectl exec -n artemis-uc4 $(kubectl get pod -n artemis-uc4 -l app=lunar-rover-telemetry -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}') -- python3 -c "import urllib.request as u; [u.urlopen(u.Request('http://127.0.0.1:8000/leak', method='POST'), timeout=2).read() for _ in range(30)]" 2>/dev/null || true

steps.title: Mission status check — is the fleet on its feet?
steps.explanation:
Three subsystems went up together, the rover just pushed a stream of telemetry, and the pad shift starts soon. Take a single look at the fleet's pods — give the kubelet ~30 s to settle, then read the `STATUS` and `RESTARTS` columns across all three workloads at once.

Three separate friction signals on three separate workloads, all visible in one listing — that is the cue to call the coordinator in. No single specialist would be enough; we need the agent that knows how to fan out.

steps.title: Ask the coordinator to diagnose the fleet
steps.explanation:
The fleet is in three different states of trouble at once and the pad shift is on the clock. Hand the cross-subsystem **diagnosis** to **`artemis-mission-coordinator`** from your terminal — its job in this beat is to fan out to its specialists in parallel, collect their verdicts, and paint your three status bulbs accordingly.

Before running the command, open your light-manager UI in a separate browser tab so you can watch your three status bulbs while the coordinator works. Workshop-infrastructure pre-wired the URL into your environment; append `?user=<your-login>` to scope which bulbs are yours (the same `?user=` query parameter your custom MCP enforces per-vCluster, so the coordinator can never paint someone else's bulbs). Once you can see your three bulbs (initially off), come back here.

The coordinator delegates to two specialists — slots 1+2 go to kagent's built-in `k8s-agent`, slot 3 goes to `artemis-rover-telemetry-debugger` (the one you applied in UC3, now reused via A2A). Each returns a verdict; the coordinator maps verdict-to-colour, writes all three bulb slots through the bulb MCP, and replies with a structured summary in your terminal.

The exact prompt the coordinator receives is surfaced here for transparency:

> Run a fleet-wide diagnosis on the Artemis subsystems active in the artemis-uc4 namespace and broadcast each verdict to my status bulbs.

**Expected end state of this beat:** all three bulbs flip red (active anomaly on each subsystem). The coordinator's terminal reply names the root cause per slot and a remediation hint per slot — read those hints before moving on, because the next beat asks the coordinator to ACT on them.

commands.label: Ask the coordinator to diagnose
commands.command: kagent invoke --agent artemis-mission-coordinator --namespace kagent --task 'Run a fleet-wide diagnosis on the Artemis subsystems active in the artemis-uc4 namespace and broadcast each verdict to my status bulbs.'

steps.title: Ask the coordinator to fix what it found
steps.explanation:
The coordinator has just diagnosed three active anomalies and your three bulbs are red. Now ask it to **fix** the fleet — same coordinator, same A2A wiring, but this time the specialists run their remediation paths instead of just reading state.

For each slot, the matching specialist applies the minimum-impact patch:

- **Slot 1 (`mission-control-imagepull`).** `k8s-agent` patches the Deployment's image to the most-recently-published tag of the same image (e.g. `:v1.0.0` instead of `:v999`). The kubelet immediately re-attempts the pull and the pod comes up.
- **Slot 2 (`mission-control-pending`).** `k8s-agent` removes the synthetic `artemis.kagent.dev/launch-pad-fault` taint from the cluster's nodes. The kube-scheduler immediately binds the Pending Pod and the pod comes up.
- **Slot 3 (`lunar-rover-telemetry`).** `artemis-rover-telemetry-debugger` patches the rover Deployment's `resources.limits.memory` upward (typically 128Mi). The new pod has more headroom; the OOM stops happening. **Important caveat the agent will surface in its reply:** raising the memory limit *hides* a memory leak in the application code, it doesn't fix it. A real fix lives in `apps/lunar-rover-telemetry/` (the `POST /leak` handler), not in the manifest. The workshop accepts the workaround as a teaching moment about agent-driven remediation; production would require a real code fix.

Watch your bulb panel while the coordinator works — slots flip from red to green as each specialist confirms its patch. The coordinator's terminal reply summarises what was patched per slot, including the rover's workaround caveat.

The exact prompt the coordinator receives:

> Now remediate every red subsystem in artemis-uc4 by delegating the fix to the matching specialist. Update my bulbs as you go.

commands.label: Ask the coordinator to remediate
commands.command: kagent invoke --agent artemis-mission-coordinator --namespace kagent --task 'Now remediate every red subsystem in artemis-uc4 by delegating the fix to the matching specialist. Update my bulbs as you go.'

steps.title: Verify + what we'd have done by hand
steps.explanation:
Confirm the fleet is healthy. The three pods should now be `Running 1/1` (the rover's restart count stays at whatever it was after the OOM cycle — that's history, not a current friction).

```bash
kubectl get pods -n artemis-uc4
```

Expected:

```
mission-control-imagepull-…   1/1   Running
mission-control-pending-…     1/1   Running
lunar-rover-telemetry-…       1/1   Running   1   (restart count from the original OOM — bulb is green)
```

**Without the coordinator, you would have walked three separate diagnostic chains and then run three separate remediations and then made three separate bulb writes**. Four surfaces, seven syntheses to do yourself.

*Diagnose:*

```bash
# Slot 1
kubectl describe pod -n artemis-uc4 -l app=mission-control-imagepull
kubectl get events -n artemis-uc4 --field-selector reason=Failed
# Slot 2
kubectl describe pod -n artemis-uc4 -l app=mission-control-pending
kubectl describe node $(kubectl get nodes -o name | head -1)
# Slot 3
kubectl describe pod -n artemis-uc4 -l app=lunar-rover-telemetry
# + open Grafana, build a memory panel for the rover's restart window
```

*Remediate:*

```bash
kubectl set image deploy/mission-control-imagepull -n artemis-uc4 mission-control=rg.fr-par.scw.cloud/apogasa/mission-control:v1.0.0
kubectl taint nodes --all artemis.kagent.dev/launch-pad-fault-
kubectl set resources deploy/lunar-rover-telemetry -n artemis-uc4 --limits=memory=128Mi
```

*Bulb writes:*

```bash
for slot in 1 2 3; do
  curl -X PUT "$LIGHT_MANAGER_URL/api/bulbs/$slot?user=$WORKSHOP_PARTICIPANT_LOGIN" \
    -H 'content-type: application/json' \
    -d '{"r":0,"g":255,"b":0}'
done
```

The coordinator did all of that in two asks — diagnose, then fix. It used the same `kubectl` tools (read + mutate) through `k8s-agent`, the same observability stack through `artemis-rover-telemetry-debugger`'s A2A delegation, and the same bulb API through the per-vCluster custom MCP — only it correlated them across three workloads in two synthesis steps. The per-vCluster MCP enforced the tenancy guarantee on every call so the coordinator could not have painted someone else's panel even if it had wanted to.

What you actually did: one `kubectl get pods`, one browser-tab open for the bulb panel, two questions to the coordinator. That's the workshop's climax — multi-agent fan-out with custom MCPs as the medium, both for status broadcast and for cross-specialist remediation. Multiplied across the dozen incidents an on-call rotation handles in a week, especially when each spans multiple subsystems with their own specialists, that's the value the workshop is selling.
